fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:

fr:title.text: Dependent path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: displayed path objects provide an unbiased and heterogeneous account of identifications between elements of different fibers of B;
left-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a forward transport operation;
right-cloven families of path objects reduce heterogeneous identification to homogeneous identification by means of a backward transport operation.

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: All of these notions are useful: because it is unbiased (i.e. prefers neither forward nor backward transport), the displayed representation is the most fundamental; on the other hand, the biased right- and left-cloven representations are the most frequently used. The fact that the latter are described directly for families of path objects in the existing sense is practically important for the compositionality of reasoning in univalent foundations, as we will more frequently have general-purpose building blocks for composing path objects than for composing displayed path objects.

fr:title.text: Displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: We will write \mathsf {DispPathObj}\,\mathcal {A} for the (large) type of displayed path objects over \mathcal {A}.

fr:title.text: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The total path object of a displayed path object

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let \mathcal {A} be a path object, and let \mathcal {B} be a displayed path object over \mathcal {A}. Then the total reflexive graph \mathcal {A}.\mathcal {B} is a path object.
Univalence is established in two singleton inductions, first using univalence of \mathcal {A} followed by univalence of \mathcal {B}.

fr:title.text: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The intrinsic left cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Given a type A and a family of path objects \mathcal {B}\colon A\to \mathsf {PathObj}, we can always define a left cleaving of \mathcal {B} over the intrinsic path object \mathord {\vartriangle }A. The left cleaving is obtained directly from the transport operation of the identification type.
We next show how right-cloven and left-cloven families of path objects both give rise to appropriate displayed path objects on their base.

fr:title.text: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of left-cloven families of path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be p_*b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} b';

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Univalence is easily established using the univalence of each \mathcal {B} x.

fr:title.text: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Display of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x\approx _{\mathcal {A}} y, we define b\approx _{\mathcal {B}}^{p} b' to be b \approx _{\mathcal {B} y} p^*b';

fr:title.text: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Covariant sum of a left-cloven family of path objects

fr:title.text: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Contravariant sum of right-cloven families of path objects

fr:title.text: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Path object structure on reflexive graphs

fr:title.text: Path object structure on fibers

fr:ol.fr:li: for p\colon x = y and \phi :fy=b, we define p^*b\colon fx=b to be identification \mathsf {act}_{f}p\cdot \phi ;
the unit law can be seen to hold using the unit laws for composition of identification.

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for sectionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon A \to B be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon s\sim s'\colon B\to A and a witness h\colon f\circ s' \sim 1_{B} that s' is a section of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon f \circ s \sim 1_{B} that s is a section of f as follows:

fr:title.text: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: The standard cleaving for retractionhood witnesses

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: Let f\colon B \to A be a given function. Given a homotopy H\colon r\sim r'\colon A\to B and a witness h\colon r'\circ f \sim 1_{B} that r' is a retraction of f, we may construct a witness H^*h\colon r \circ f \sim 1_{B} that r is a retraction of f as follows:

fr:title.text: Path object structure on sections
Path object structure on retractions
Path object structure on retractions
Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:frontmatter.fr:title: Right cleaving of a family of path objects

fr:title.text: Dependent product of path objects

fr:mainmatter.fr:p: The product of a family \mathcal {B}\colon A \to \mathsf {PathObj} path objects is a path object.
Univalence follows from the closure of families of contractible types under dependent products (Lemma ) via the distributivity of \prod over \sum .

fr:title.text: Section-retraction pair
